title: Ranunculus isophylloides
intro: The Ranunculus isophylloides, also known as the Mountain Buttercup, is a perennial plant species that belongs to the buttercup family (Ranunculaceae). This plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various health issues, including inflammation and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Ranunculus isophylloides include alkaloids, glycosides, and flavonoids, which are responsible for its therapeutic effects. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared as a decoction, tincture, or infusion, and is commonly used to treat conditions such as eczema, acne, and wounds.
taxonomy: Ranunculus isophylloides is a species of flowering plant in the family Ranunculaceae. This plant is native to the Mediterranean region and is commonly found in areas with alkaline soils. The classification of Ranunculus isophylloides places it within the genus Ranunculus, which contains around 600 species of plants. The family Ranunculaceae is a group of flowering plants that also includes buttercups, clematis, and anemones. Ranunculus isophylloides is often used as an ornamental plant in gardens due to its attractive flowers and ability to thrive in a variety of conditions.

side_effects: Ranunculus isophylloides, also known as the yellow-flowered ranunculus, contains toxic compounds called ranunculin and protoanemonin, which can be irritating to the skin and mucous membranes. When ingested, these compounds can cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. In severe cases, ranunculin can cause cardiac arrest and renal failure. There is limited information available on the long-term effects of exposure to Ranunculus isophylloides. Handling the plant requires caution and protective gear to avoid skin contact and potential irritation.
